fabricated_divergences.quote: German-language coverage (Tagesschau, DW) foregrounds government officials' statements and structural/economic analysis (police costs, youth unemployment, historical parallels to Ghana and Uganda expulsions), while French-language coverage (RFI, France 24) foregrounds affected migrants' visceral fear and testimony, such as a pastor saying refugees no longer fear death and a taxi driver describing constant unease.
fabricated_divergences.why_unsupported: The historical parallels to Ghana (1969) and Uganda (1972) expulsions appear only in article 8, which is AllAfrica (US, English), not in any German-language coverage. Tagesschau (article 6) contains government/movement statements about the deadline but no structural/economic analysis or historical parallels; the unemployment/police-cost material is in DW (article 3), which is an English-language outlet, not German-language. This claim contradicts the candidate's own divergence 5 crediting AllAfrica uniquely with the historical frame.

fabricated_divergences.quote: European outlets (DW, Tagesschau, RFI) quote or feature civil society and academic analysts (Tessa Dooms, Fredson Guilengue, Vanya Gastrow) who contextualize the crisis within governance failures, whereas Qatar-based Al Jazeera centers ground-level reporting from informal settlements and markets, prioritizing affected-community testimony over expert analysis.
fabricated_divergences.why_unsupported: Vanya Gastrow is quoted in article 8, which is AllAfrica (US), not a European outlet, so grouping her with European coverage is unsupported. Moreover, Al Jazeera article 7 explicitly features Professor Luke Sinwell of the University of Johannesburg providing academic analysis, directly contradicting the claim that Al Jazeera prioritizes affected-community testimony over expert analysis.
